theory.intro: When an object is dropped into water, the water level rises. The **rise** in the water level equals the **volume** of the object.
facts: $1\text{ mL}$ of water takes up exactly $1\text{ cm}^3$ of space.
A bigger rise means a bigger object; the same rise means equal volumes.
steps: Read the water level **before** the object goes in.
Read the water level **after** the object is fully submerged.
Subtract: the rise is the object's volume.

foundation.q: The water rose from $40$ mL to $55$ mL when a stone was dropped in. What is the stone's volume?
The water rose from $20$ mL to $30$ mL. What is the object's volume?
The water rose from $50$ mL to $70$ mL. What is the object's volume?
The water rose from $60$ mL to $85$ mL. What is the object's volume?
The water rose from $30$ mL to $36$ mL. What is the object's volume?
The water rose from $10$ mL to $45$ mL. What is the object's volume?
Before: $25$ mL. After: $40$ mL. Find the object's volume.
Before: $80$ mL. After: $92$ mL. Find the object's volume.
Before: $15$ mL. After: $50$ mL. Find the object's volume.
Before: $100$ mL. After: $128$ mL. Find the object's volume.

development.q: A marble raises the level from $50$ mL to $58$ mL. A second identical marble is added. What is the new level?
Object $A$ raises the level by $12$ mL; object $B$ raises it by $18$ mL. Which has the greater volume, and by how much?
The level went from $40$ mL to $76$ mL when $3$ identical beads were dropped in. What is the volume of one bead?
A toy raises the level from $200$ mL to $245$ mL. Give its volume in mL and in $\text{cm}^3$.
Two objects together raise the level by $30$ mL. One has volume $11$ mL. What is the other's volume?
A rock raises the level from $120$ mL to $165$ mL. A second rock raises it a further $25$ mL. What is the final level?
The water level rose from $35$ mL to $35$ mL when a cork floated. Explain the object's volume reading.
development.a: No reliable reading — it floats, not submerged

development.q: An object raises the level from $60$ mL to $96$ mL. A copy of the object would raise an empty trial from $60$ mL to what?
Reading the cylinder: before $80$ mL, after $110$ mL. Find the object's volume.
Object $P$ raises the level by $20$ mL, object $Q$ by $20$ mL. Compare their volumes.

mastery.q: A measuring jug reads $250$ mL. After a fruit is added it reads $0.31$ L. What is the fruit's volume in mL?
Five identical screws raise the level from $40$ mL to $65$ mL. What is the volume of one screw?
A stone has volume $48$ mL. The cylinder starts at $90$ mL and overflows past $120$ mL. Will it overflow?
mastery.a: Yes — needs $138$ mL but only $120$ mL fits

mastery.q: Object $X$ raised the level by twice as much as object $Y$. $Y$ raised it from $50$ mL to $62$ mL. By how much would $X$ raise it?
A cube of side $3$ cm is submerged. By how many mL does the water rise?
After a toy is added the level is $88$ mL; the toy's volume is $33$ mL. What was the starting level?
Two objects are tested. $A$: $30\to48$ mL. $B$: $48\to70$ mL (added after $A$). Order their volumes.
A pebble of volume $14$ mL and a marble of volume $6$ mL are both dropped into a cylinder at $70$ mL. What is the new level?
Object $M$ raises a $1$ L cylinder from $0.4$ L to $0.46$ L. Give its volume in mL.
A crown raises the level from $200$ mL to $290$ mL. An equal mass of pure gold would raise it only $80$ mL. Do they have equal volume?
mastery.a: No — crown is $90$ mL, gold is $80$ mL
